structured_payload_candidate.title: Control Valve Pressure Drop for Complementary Characteristic
structured_payload_candidate.summary: In variable-speed pumping with differential pressure control, the valve pressure drop for control is that required to provide complementary characteristic.

evidence_text:
13.16 2024 ASHRAE Handbook—HV AC Systems and Equipment (SI)
ures 25 and 26). Typically, authorities in the range of 30 to 50% (for
piping systems with 2: 1 branch-to-riser pressure drop ratios) are
adequate for HV AC coil applications, with higher t coils requiring
less valve authority. Authorities less than 30% should be avoided
because they lead to a flow characteristic shift that makes the equal-percentage valve appear to be more linear in nature (Figures 27 and
28). For the three-way valve shown in Figure 23, the full-open pres-
sure drop should be from C to D.  The pressure drop in the bypass
balancing valve in the three-way valv e circuit should be set to equal
that in the coil (load), Chapter 47 discusses valve authority in threeway modulating applications.
Control valves should be sized on the basis of the required valve
coefficient C
v for the required pressure drop and flow. For more
information, see the section on Control Valve Sizing under Auto-
matic Valves in Chapter 47.
judge_reason: The evidence provided does not discuss variable-speed pumping or complementary characteristic; it is unsupported.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Valve Authority for Good Results with 6.7 K Δt Coil
structured_payload_candidate.summary: For coils with a 6.7 K Δt, valve authority of 50 to 100% provides good results, implying a pressure drop on the valve equal to that of the coil.

evidence_text:
13.16 2024 ASHRAE Handbook—HV AC Systems and Equipment (SI)
ures 25 and 26). Typically, authorities in the range of 30 to 50% (for

v for the required pressure drop and flow. For more

matic Valves in Chapter 47.
judge_reason: The evidence does not mention a 6.7 K Δt coil or authority of 50-100%; this detail is not present.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Low-Temperature Heating System Supply Temperatures
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Low-temperature heating systems are generally designed with supply water temperatures from 80 to 115°C and temperature drops from 10 to 55 K.

evidence_text:

structured_payload_candidate.title: Historical US Hot-Water Heating Design Temperatures
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Historically, US hot-water heating systems were designed for a 93°C supply water temperature and an 11 K temperature drop.

evidence_text:
In these valves, attention must also be paid to valve con-
struction (especially the maximu m pressure allowed on the valve
body, and maximum allowed pres sure drop across the valve).
If a system is to be designed wi th multiple zones of control such
that load response is to be by constant flow through the load andvariable t, control cannot be achieved  by valve control alone; a
load pump is required.
Several control arrangements of  load pump and control valve
configurations are shown in Figur e 29. Note that, in all three
configurations, the common pipe ha s no restriction or check valve.
In all configurations, there is no difference in control as seen by theload.
judge_reason: The evidence is about valve construction and load pump configurations, not historical US hot-water design temperatures.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Coil Rating Parameters per AHRI Standard 410
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Dry-surface and dehumidifying coils are rated within parameters including entering air temperatures, air face velocity, and water velocity.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Standard Air Density for Airflow Ratings
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Airflow ratings are based on standard air of 1.20 kg/m³ at 20°C and a barometric pressure of 101.325 kPa.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Airflow Resistance Difference Between Dry and Dehumidifying Coils
structured_payload_candidate.summary: A completely dry coil, removing only sensible heat, offers approximately one-third less resistance to airflow than a dehumidifying coil removing both sensible and latent heat.

evidence_text:
